ontology.foodex2_description: Pork meat products — mixed/prepared pork dish
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for pork head salad; A0CKP (pork meat products) used as closest defensible category anchor.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Pork Head Salad is approximately 150 g, equivalent to one small plate as commonly served in Singapore hawker or Burmese restaurant settings.

nutrient_source.title: Pork Head Salad (M18) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Foodo entry for M18 Pork Head Salad (ဝက်ခေါင်းသုပ်), cr_id G00010952, with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat not reported by HPB; estimated from USDA FoodData Central pork head meat composition data as a supplementary reference.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on mixed pork and vegetable salad with fish sauce dressing
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Pork Head Salad. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on analogous mixed meat-and-vegetable salad with fish sauce dressing from the University of Sydney GI Database. The dish is predominantly fat and protein with only 5.9 g/100 g carbohydrate, which suppresses postprandial glucose response. GL per 150 g serving = (45 × 5.9 × 1.5) / 100 ≈ 4.0 g, classified low.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 810 mg/100 g — well above the 600 mg/100 g high-concern threshold; fish sauce is the primary driver with potassium only 64 mg/100 g providing negligible offset.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit Pork Head Salad; a 150 g serving delivers approximately 1215 mg sodium, representing over 50% of the 2300 mg daily limit, and the low potassium-to-sodium ratio further impairs blood pressure regulation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Extremely high fat content (44.5 g/100 g) with zero dietary fibre increases caloric density and promotes insulin resistance; high sodium further compounds metabolic risk.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid Pork Head Salad due to its very high fat load (predominantly saturated fat from pork skin), absence of dietary fibre, and high sodium content, all of which worsen insulin sensitivity and cardiovascular co-morbidity risk.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (~12 g/100 g estimated) from pork skin and head meat raises LDL cholesterol; sodium 810 mg/100 g elevates blood pressure; cholesterol 26 mg/100 g adds additional atherogenic burden.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid Pork Head Salad; the combination of high saturated fat, high sodium, and dietary cholesterol significantly increases LDL-driven atherosclerosis risk and hypertensive load, and a single 150 g serving exceeds recommended saturated fat intake for the day.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pork (red meat) is rich in arachidonic acid amplifying pro-inflammatory cytokines; pork skin contributes very high saturated fat; sodium 810 mg/100 g worsens RA-related tissue oedema; chilli is a nightshade.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid Pork Head Salad; red meat arachidonic acid, high saturated fat from pork skin, and nightshade chilli collectively amplify systemic inflammation and joint swelling, while the high sodium content worsens fluid retention in inflamed tissues.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pork Head Salad (ဝက်ခေါင်းသုပ်, romanized: Wak Kaung Thoke) is a Burmese-origin cold salad dish served in Singapore's hawker and Burmese restaurant settings. It is prepared by boiling pork head meat and pork skin until tender, slicing them thinly, and tossing with a pungent dressing of fish sauce, lime juice, chilli, lemongrass, ginger, shallots, and garlic, then garnished with crushed peanuts and toasted sesame seeds. The texture is a contrast of gelatinous, tender pork skin and firm sliced meat, with a bold flavour profile that is simultaneously salty, sour, spicy, and nutty. Nutritionally, the dish is extremely high in fat at 44.5 g per 100 g, predominantly saturated fat from pork skin, with very low protein at 1.32 g per 100 g and minimal carbohydrate at 5.9 g per 100 g. Sodium is very high at 810 mg per 100 g, driven primarily by fish sauce. Dietary fibre is absent. These characteristics make it clinically unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis. The dish is not halal due to pork content and contains peanut and sesame allergens. It is typically consumed as a starter or side dish at lunch or dinner. The HPB Singapore Foodo database lists this dish under cr_id G00010952 with high confidence (0.95).
common_user_queries: Can diabetics eat pork head salad?
Is pork head salad suitable for hypertension?
What is ဝက်ခေါင်းသုပ်?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for M18 Pork Head Salad (cr_id G00010952) with a reported confidence of 0.95. Source match is exact. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central pork head composition as a supplementary reference since HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this traditional Burmese pork head salad preparation are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. 2. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB Foodo source entry. 3. saturated_fat_g of 12.0 g/100 g is an estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central pork head meat and skin composition data (USDA FDC ID 168319 and related entries) and is flagged as approximate. 4. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this dish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 5. The input record incorrectly classified the cuisine as 'Western'; corrected to 'Burmese / Southeast Asian' based on dish name, Burmese-script alias, and ingredient profile. 6. protein_g (1.32) and carbohydrate_g (5.9) are non-zero values from the HPB source; no zero-macro justification required for these fields. fat_g (44.54) is non-zero. All core macros are present and non-zero.
